era_context: The late 14th century is marked by the Hundred Years' War between England and France, the Ottoman Empire's expansion in Anatolia and the Balkans, the Ming Dynasty's consolidation in China, and the early Italian Renaissance. The Catholic Church remains unified but experiencing tensions that would lead to the Western Schism in 1378.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Interior Americas (Aztec and Inca records exist but are sparse in European sources)

events.title: Battle of La Rochelle; English naval defeat
events.description: On 22 June 1372, an English naval fleet was defeated by a Castilian-French fleet at La Rochelle. The battle was a significant naval engagement in the Hundred Years' War, resulting in the loss of English ships and personnel. The defeat marked a turning point in English naval dominance and demonstrated the growing power of French and Castilian naval forces.
key_figures: John Hastings, Earl of Pembroke (English commander, killed in battle)

sources.name: Froissart's Chronicles; English and French naval records
Historiographic consensus on Hundred Years' War naval battles

events.certainty_note: The Battle of La Rochelle is well-documented in contemporary chronicles and naval records. The date 22 June 1372 is standard in all major sources.

events.title: Ming Dynasty: consolidation of power; administrative centralization
events.description: The Hongwu Emperor continued consolidating Ming power in 1372 through administrative reforms and the centralization of authority. He was establishing the bureaucratic structures and systems of control that would define Ming governance. This period saw the elimination of regional power centers and the concentration of power in the imperial court.

sources.name: Ming Shilu (Veritable Records of the Ming)

events.certainty_note: The Hongwu Emperor's administrative reforms are well-documented in Ming records, though specific reforms in 1372 require careful reading of the Ming Shilu.

events.title: Ottoman expansion in Anatolia; consolidation of Murad I's power
events.description: Murad I, the Ottoman Sultan, continued expanding Ottoman territory in Anatolia in 1372. The Ottoman state was consolidating its power and establishing itself as a major regional force. This period saw the Ottoman Empire transition from a small principality to a significant power in the eastern Mediterranean and Balkans.
events.certainty_note: Ottoman expansion in 1372 is attested in Ottoman sources, but specific events and dates can vary. The broader pattern of Murad I's consolidation is well-established.

events.title: Tamerlane's consolidation of power in Central Asia
events.description: Timur (Tamerlane) was consolidating his power in Central Asia in 1372, eliminating rival khans and establishing the foundation of the Timurid Empire. He was conducting military campaigns and diplomatic negotiations to establish his authority over Transoxiana and neighboring regions.

sources.name: Timurid chronicles (Yazdi, Sharaf al-Din Ali Yazdi)

events.certainty_note: Timur's consolidation of power is well-attested in Islamic and Persian sources, though specific events in 1372 are not always clearly dated.

events.title: Petrarch's continued influence on Italian humanism
events.description: In 1372, Petrarch was in his final years, continuing to write and influence the intellectual life of Italy. His works on classical texts and human achievement were shaping the development of humanism. His correspondence and writings from this period demonstrate his continued engagement with intellectual and political matters.
events.certainty_note: Petrarch's activities in 1372 are documented in his surviving writings and correspondence, though the specific events of this year are not always clearly recorded.

disconfirming_evidence: The Battle of La Rochelle is sometimes portrayed as a decisive turning point in English naval power, but English naval dominance was not permanently lost. English fleets continued to operate effectively in subsequent years. The significance of the battle may be exaggerated in some accounts. Ottoman and Central Asian expansion in 1372 is attested in sources, but the exact significance of specific events is sometimes disputed.
historiographic_note: 1372 is well-documented for Western Europe, the Ottoman Empire, and China. Italian sources on Petrarch and humanism are abundant. Ottoman and Central Asian sources exist but are often later compilations. The year is less well-documented for regions outside these areas. The surviving record emphasizes political and military events; economic and cultural history require inference from broader patterns.

graph_edges.note: English naval defeat at La Rochelle in 1372 did not prevent continued English military campaigns in 1373
Petrarch's continued influence in 1372 extended into his final year in 1373
